questions.question: A radioactive isotope decays according to the formula N(t) = N₀ · (1/2)^(t/h), where h is the half-life in years. If an isotope has a half-life of 12 years and currently 375 mg remains, how many milligrams were present 36 years ago?
questions.explanation: Work backwards: N(t) = N₀ · (1/2)^(t/h), so 375 = N₀ · (1/2)^(36/12) = N₀ · (1/2)³ = N₀/8. Therefore N₀ = 375 × 8 = 3000 mg.

questions.question: An investment grows according to A(t) = P(1.05)^t, where t is in years. If you want your $5,000 investment to triple, approximately how many years must you wait? (Express as a logarithmic equation you must solve.)
questions.explanation: Set 3P = P(1.05)^t, so 3 = (1.05)^t. Taking logarithms: t·log(1.05) = log(3), giving t = log(3)/log(1.05) ≈ 22.5 years.

questions.question: A bacterial colony doubles every 3 hours. If the colony currently contains 2^13 bacteria, how many bacteria were present 12 hours ago?
questions.explanation: In 12 hours, the colony doubles 4 times (12÷3=4). Going backward 4 doublings means dividing by 2⁴. So 2^13 ÷ 2⁴ = 2^9 = 512.

questions.question: The value of a car depreciates exponentially: V(t) = V₀ · e^(-kt), where t is in years. A car worth $20,000 is worth $12,000 after 5 years. Find k (the decay constant) to two decimal places.
A social media post goes viral with engagement following N(t) = 500 · 2^(t/2), where t is hours after posting. At what time will engagement reach 16,000?
questions.explanation: Set 16,000 = 500 · 2^(t/2). Divide: 32 = 2^(t/2). Since 32 = 2⁵, we have t/2 = 5, so t = 8 hours.

questions.question: Compound interest formula: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt). If you invest $2,000 at 8% annual interest compounded quarterly, will your balance exceed $5,000 within 15 years? Show your reasoning.
